recommendations.rationale: For uncomplicated acute LBP (<6 weeks) without red flags, imaging does not change management and may lead to overtreatment. Conservative management is first-line.
recommendations.india_context: Imaging in non-specific LBP shows findings in >80% of asymptomatic adults — disc bulge, facet arthropathy. These are age-related, not necessarily the cause of pain.
recommendations.special_notes: Red flags requiring urgent imaging: age >50, fever, weight loss, history of malignancy, IV drug use, trauma, neurological deficit, sphincter dysfunction, steroid use.

recommendations.rationale: Limited value in non-specific LBP. May detect spondylolisthesis, fracture, or spondylitis if clinically suspected.
recommendations.india_context: Not recommended for routine acute LBP. Order only if red flags present (fracture, spondyloarthropathy suspicion).

recommendations.rationale: Not indicated in acute non-specific LBP. Reserve for red flags or when radiculopathy requires surgical evaluation.
recommendations.india_context: High rate of incidental findings leading to unnecessary procedures. Do not order routinely.
recommendations.special_notes: Indicated for: suspected cauda equina syndrome (EMERGENCY), neurological deficits, cancer workup, infection, >6 weeks with failed conservative treatment.

recommendations.rationale: Not recommended for non-specific LBP. High radiation dose. MRI preferred when imaging needed.
recommendations.india_context: Avoid routine CT for LBP. Use only for bony pathology (fracture, bony metastasis) or when MRI contraindicated.

recommendations.rationale: First-line investigation. Identifies most fractures, dislocations, and soft tissue calcification. Minimum two perpendicular views essential.
recommendations.india_context: Always request specific anatomical views — wrist (PA + lateral), ankle (AP + lateral + mortise), etc. Ottawa rules guide clinical use.
recommendations.special_notes: Ottawa Ankle Rules and Ottawa Knee Rules help decide if X-ray is needed — reduces unnecessary imaging.

recommendations.rationale: For complex fractures — intra-articular (calcaneum, tibial plateau, acetabulum), occult fractures not seen on X-ray, pre-operative planning.
recommendations.india_context: Essential for surgical planning in complex joint fractures. CT with 3D reconstruction for acetabular and calcaneal fractures.

recommendations.rationale: For occult fractures (hip stress fracture, scaphoid, undisplaced tibial plateau) not visible on X-ray.
recommendations.india_context: Order when X-ray normal but clinical suspicion high — especially hip fractures in elderly (high morbidity if missed).
recommendations.special_notes: MRI detects bone marrow oedema — most sensitive for occult fracture.

recommendations.rationale: Alternative when MRI unavailable for occult fracture detection. Whole body — useful for multifocal injury or metastasis.
recommendations.india_context: Use when MRI unavailable. Positive within 24–72 hours of fracture.
recommendations.special_notes: Less specific than MRI. False positives in infection, arthritis, tumour.

scenarios.title: Knee Pain — Acute (Suspected Ligament / Meniscal Injury)

recommendations.rationale: First-line to exclude fracture (Ottawa Knee Rules), dislocation, loose bodies, and to assess joint space.
recommendations.india_context: Apply Ottawa Knee Rules. X-ray if: age >55, isolated patella tenderness, head of fibula tenderness, cannot flex knee 90°, cannot weight bear.
recommendations.special_notes: Cannot visualise soft tissue (ligaments, meniscus). MRI required for soft tissue.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for soft tissue assessment — ACL, PCL, MCL, LCL, menisci, articular cartilage, and bone marrow oedema.
recommendations.india_context: Indicated when soft tissue injury suspected and X-ray normal, or before arthroscopy. 3T preferred but 1.5T acceptable.
recommendations.special_notes: High sensitivity and specificity for ACL tears (>90%). Guides surgical decision-making.

recommendations.rationale: For superficial structures — Baker's cyst, quadriceps/patellar tendon tears, collateral ligaments. Cannot assess cruciate ligaments or menisci reliably.
recommendations.india_context: Useful for tendon assessment and guided aspiration of joint effusion. Operator dependent.

recommendations.rationale: Investigation of choice. Most sensitive and specific for osteomyelitis. Detects bone marrow involvement, soft tissue extension, and abscess.
recommendations.india_context: Particularly valuable for spinal and diabetic foot osteomyelitis. Gadolinium contrast enhances accuracy.
recommendations.special_notes: High sensitivity (>90%) and specificity (>85%). Can differentiate Charcot foot from osteomyelitis.

recommendations.rationale: First-line. Changes appear 10–21 days after onset — periosteal reaction, lytic areas, sclerosis. Early osteomyelitis may be normal.
recommendations.india_context: Always start with plain X-ray. Normal X-ray in acute setting requires further imaging.
recommendations.special_notes: Bony destruction visible only after 30–50% mineral loss. X-ray normal in early stages.

recommendations.rationale: Positive within 24–48 hours of onset. Three-phase bone scan increases specificity. Useful when MRI contraindicated.
recommendations.india_context: Alternative when MRI not available. Whole body scan for multifocal disease.
recommendations.special_notes: WBC scan (labelled leucocytes) more specific for osteomyelitis than bone scan alone.

recommendations.rationale: For cortical destruction, sequestrum, involucrum in chronic osteomyelitis. Guides surgical drainage.
recommendations.india_context: Use for detailed bony architecture in chronic disease or pre-surgical planning.

recommendations.rationale: First-line for rotator cuff evaluation. Dynamic assessment of supraspinatus, infraspinatus, subscapularis, biceps tendon. High sensitivity and specificity for full-thickness tears (>92%). Cost-effective and widely available.
recommendations.india_context: Most cost-effective option in India for rotator cuff tears. Dynamic US is superior to static MRI for impingement assessment. Available at most physiotherapy and orthopaedic units.
recommendations.special_notes: Full-thickness tear: non-visualisation of tendon. Partial tear: focal hypoechoic defect. Calcific tendinitis: hyperechoic foci with shadowing. Dynamic impingement best assessed with US.

recommendations.rationale: Superior to US for partial-thickness tears, labral pathology, SLAP lesions, Hill-Sachs/Bankart lesions (instability). MRI arthrogram (MRA) has highest sensitivity for partial tears and labral lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Use when US is inconclusive, instability workup is needed, or surgical planning is required. MRI arthrogram mandatory for labral tear assessment.
recommendations.special_notes: MRI arthrogram (direct intraarticular gadolinium): sensitivity >90% for partial articular-surface tears and labral tears. Essential for instability (Bankart, Hill-Sachs lesion).
contraindicated_if: recent intraarticular injection (>4 weeks delay for arthrogram)

recommendations.rationale: First-line for acute trauma or when calcific tendinitis, acromioclavicular joint disease, glenohumeral arthritis, or dislocation is suspected. Identifies ACJ, acromion morphology, calcification.
recommendations.india_context: Cheap and widely available. Must be first investigation in trauma. Axillary view mandatory to confirm reduction post-dislocation.
recommendations.special_notes: Supraspinatus outlet view shows acromion morphology (Type I/II/III Bigliani) — predicts impingement. AP + Grashey (true AP) + Axillary = minimum 3 views.

recommendations.rationale: First-line for all hip pain. Identifies fractures, arthritis, AVN changes (crescent sign, collapse, sclerosis), bone tumours, and Perthes disease. Essential before any further imaging.
recommendations.india_context: AVN in India commonly caused by steroid use (nephrotic syndrome, rheumatic diseases), trauma, sickle cell disease, and alcohol. X-ray is first-line everywhere.
recommendations.special_notes: ARCO Stage I (X-ray normal, MRI positive) — MRI is needed. Crescent sign = subchondral fracture (ARCO Stage II/III). Collapse = Stage III/IV.

recommendations.rationale: Most sensitive investigation for early AVN (ARCO Stage I — before X-ray changes). Identifies bone marrow oedema, double line sign (pathognomonic for AVN), and contralateral hip disease. Bilateral imaging essential as AVN is bilateral in 50–80%.
recommendations.india_context: MRI is mandatory for early diagnosis of steroid-induced AVN. Bilateral hip MRI should be performed in all cases. Early diagnosis allows core decompression before collapse.
recommendations.special_notes: Double line sign on MRI (T2 sclerotic outer line + inner hyperintense line) = pathognomonic for AVN. STIR or fat-sat T2 for marrow oedema. Always image BOTH hips.

recommendations.rationale: Cold spot (photopenic area) in femoral head = early AVN. Less sensitive and specific than MRI. Use when MRI unavailable.
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine departments. Use as MRI alternative when unavailable. Can screen bilateral hips in one study.
recommendations.special_notes: Cold-on-hot pattern (photopenic centre with hyperaemic rim) = reparative phase of AVN.

recommendations.rationale: First-line for cervical spondylosis. Identifies disc space narrowing, osteophytes, foraminal narrowing (oblique views), and instability (flexion-extension views). Not for acute disc prolapse.
recommendations.india_context: Cervical spondylosis is extremely common in India. X-ray of cervical spine is first-line. Degenerative changes often seen from the 4th decade onwards.
recommendations.special_notes: Oblique views show foraminal narrowing (nerve root impingement level). Flexion-extension views for instability (atlantoaxial subluxation, rheumatoid arthritis).

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for cervical radiculopathy and myelopathy. Identifies disc prolapse, cord compression, nerve root compression, foraminal stenosis, and cord signal change (myelopathy). Essential for surgical planning.
recommendations.india_context: MRI is the definitive investigation for patients not responding to conservative treatment or with progressive neurological signs. Available at all major centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Cord signal change (T2 hyperintensity) = myelopathic change — surgical urgency. Neural foramina best seen on oblique parasagittal sequences. GRE sequences for ossified OPLL.

recommendations.rationale: Excellent for ossified pathology (OPLL, bony spurs, foraminal osteophytes) and post-surgical assessment. Complements MRI. Superior to MRI for bone detail.
recommendations.india_context: OPLL is a significant cause of cervical myelopathy in Indian patients. CT identifies extent of OPLL better than MRI.
recommendations.special_notes: CT myelogram if MRI is contraindicated. OPLL: continuous or segmental ossification along posterior vertebral bodies.

scenarios.title: Suspected Bone Tumour / Malignant Bone Lesion

recommendations.rationale: Mandatory first investigation. Characterises lesion (lytic/sclerotic/mixed), zone of transition, cortical destruction, periosteal reaction (Codman triangle, sunburst, onion-skin), and pathological fracture risk.
recommendations.india_context: X-ray is the cornerstone of bone tumour diagnosis. Osteosarcoma (most common primary bone malignancy in adolescents) and Ewing sarcoma are important diagnoses. Metastases from breast, prostate, lung, thyroid, kidney are most common.
recommendations.special_notes: Aggressive features: wide zone of transition, periosteal reaction (Codman triangle, sunburst pattern), soft tissue extension. Benign features: sclerotic margin, narrow zone of transition. Never biopsy before imaging.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for local staging, soft tissue extension, neurovascular involvement, and skip lesions. Essential before biopsy and surgical resection planning.
recommendations.india_context: MRI is mandatory for all suspected primary bone sarcomas. Identifies intraosseous extent, joint involvement, and growth plate crossing — determines surgical margins.
recommendations.special_notes: Intraosseous extent: T1 hypointense; Skip lesions: separate T1 hypointense foci in same bone. Soft tissue mass: best seen on T2 fat-sat or STIR. Always include the entire bone in the field of view.

recommendations.rationale: CT chest for pulmonary metastases (osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma). Whole-body CT or PET-CT for staging metastatic disease and skip lesions.
recommendations.india_context: Osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma stage with CT chest. Skip lesions in same bone are important — MRI of entire bone mandatory.
recommendations.special_notes: Osteosarcoma: pulmonary mets most common — CT chest is standard. Ewing: whole body PET-CT preferred.

recommendations.rationale: Detects skeletal metastases and polyostotic lesions. Identifies skip lesions and evaluates extent of bone involvement. Useful for metastatic workup and myeloma assessment (lytic lesions may be cold).
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine centres. Whole-body survey for metastases from common primaries (breast, prostate, lung). For myeloma, PET-CT or whole-body MRI is superior to bone scan.
recommendations.special_notes: Hot spots = increased osteoblastic activity (most metastases, osteosarcoma). Cold spots = purely lytic disease (myeloma, certain RCC mets).
